meta.note: Canonical data for the ARIA visual web. The renderer reads this file and never hardcodes node data. Spec lives in docs/aria/COGNITIVE_MANSION.md. v2 (2026-05-15): every plain_meaning rewritten in zero-jargon language — no ARIA terms inside a definition — and an emergence field added to every node ('what was happening / what was breaking when this idea emerged', one plain human sentence). A words_to_know glossary was added for terms a newcomer still would not know. why_it_exists and came_from are kept from v1 as the more technical layer. synced_at is the commit at which a node was last verified against its source file. v3 (2026-05-16): Node Understanding Spec fields added to the schema — why_caleb_cared, real_example, how_to_use_this (optional, mainly tool/rule nodes), and open_questions (array of strings) on nodes, plus an optional why on edges (a plain-sentence explanation of the relationship). Only the thesis node and the thesis->preserve_emergence edge are populated as worked examples; the other 28 nodes and 47 edges are not yet filled. A meta.origin block was added 2026-05-16 holding the reconciled project-level ARIA origin story (full evidence in docs/aria/ARIA_IDEAS.md). v4 (2026-05-16): backfilled layout data so the JSON is fully canonical — a short field (2-line node label) on every node, and fixed_position x/y filled for all 29 nodes (previously only thesis had coordinates), sourced from the embedded copy in ARIA_WEB.html. Unblocks the renderer fetch cutover.

origin.true_origin: April 29, 2026. While building Uncrumple, Caleb feared losing context, momentum, and the thread of his thinking while switching between AI tools and long sessions.
origin.first_system_response: A two-file master system to carry state forward between tools and sessions.
origin.system_origin: Handoff machinery, locked attributes, session logs, and JSON continuity matured after that.
origin.reusability_moment: 2026-05-06. The system was recognized as reusable beyond Uncrumple — closer to a DevOS.
origin.crystallization_event: The forced Windows restart (~2026-05-13/14) proved repo continuity can survive while human orientation still fails. It did not start ARIA; it revealed the next layer.
origin.plain_english: ARIA began from Caleb's fear of losing context, momentum, and meaning while switching between AI tools and long sessions. The first answer was a two-file master system for carrying state forward. That evolved into handoff machinery, then a reusable DevOS, then ARIA. The forced Windows restart came later and proved a deeper problem: files can survive while human orientation still fails.
origin.evidence: Reconciled from three independent archaeologies (Code repo dig, GPT memory, Claude conversation search), 2026-05-16. Full evidence in docs/aria/ARIA_IDEAS.md [2026-05-16 ARIA Origin Reconciliation].

words_to_know.definition: The system this whole project is building: a way to save not just your files but your sense of where you were in your own thinking, so you can pick up cleanly after a break.
One bubble on the web. One idea.
A line between two bubbles. A relationship between two ideas.
The one core idea everything else serves. There is only one. (Also one of the six categories.)
A belief about how to think or work. One of the six categories.
A concrete thing you do or use — a method or a step. One of the six categories.
A hard always-or-never; a guardrail. One of the six categories.
A named idea that describes something but is not itself an action. One of the six categories.
A future idea, not built yet, maybe not buildable yet. One of the six categories.
Short for repository: the project's folder of files plus its complete saved history of every change ever made.
One saved snapshot in that history, with a short note on what changed.
Claude, GPT, and Code — the three assistants Caleb works with. Claude and GPT plan and think; Code makes the actual file changes.
Copying one AI's answer into another AI, over and over — useful at first, but easy to keep doing past the point the question was already answered.
Squashing rich ideas into a short summary that loses the important parts. The main thing ARIA exists to prevent.
One continuous stretch of working with an AI before its memory is reset.
Passing the project's state from one session to the next so nothing is lost.
The shape of how ideas connect — which ideas link to which, and how. The 'map' part of the web.

nodes.label: Making cognition traversable without flattening it
nodes.plain_meaning: The whole point of ARIA: let a person walk back through their own thinking later without it getting squashed into a short summary that loses the parts that mattered.
nodes.emergence: Caleb kept losing the thread of his own ideas after breaks, and summaries kept throwing away the details that made the ideas worth having.
nodes.why_it_exists: Every tool that stores ideas also flattens them; ARIA exists to preserve the topology instead.
nodes.came_from: Claude's one-line synthesis, 2026-05-14, distilled from the whole session.
nodes.why_caleb_cared: Caleb kept walking away from heavy sessions and coming back to find every file intact but himself lost — he could not feel his way back into his own thinking. He cared because losing the thread of your own ideas, again and again, is exhausting and quietly discouraging. He wanted to return to the work and still recognize his own mind in it.
nodes.real_example: After the forced Windows restart, every file came back perfectly — the repo was completely intact. But Caleb sat back down not knowing what he had been doing, why it mattered, or what the next move was. The files survived; his place in the work did not. That exact gap is what 'traversable without flattening' means: the repo was traversable, the thinking was not.
nodes.how_to_use_this: Use it as the yardstick for every other ARIA idea. For anything proposed, ask: does this help a person walk back through their own thinking without it being flattened into a lossy summary? If it does not serve that, it does not belong in ARIA.
open_questions: Can 'meaning' actually be preserved in a file, or does writing anything down always flatten it a little?
Is the thesis a buildable feature, or a direction that individual features only point toward?
Where should the project-level ARIA origin story live, and does the thesis node need its own origin field?

nodes.label: Preserve emergence before compression
nodes.plain_meaning: Write down how an idea actually came about before you shorten it — because shortening too early throws away the story of how you got there.
nodes.emergence: When the AIs kept summarizing the conversation, the details and the feeling behind ideas started disappearing, and Caleb stopped recognizing his own thinking.
nodes.why_it_exists: Summaries kept flattening the session's ideas; this is the operating rule that prevents it.
nodes.came_from: GPT named it the hidden connective thesis of the session, 2026-05-14.

nodes.label: Repo continuity != Human continuity
nodes.plain_meaning: Your saved files surviving a computer restart is one thing. You actually feeling like you know where you were is a completely separate thing — and the second one is the hard, unsolved part.
nodes.emergence: A Windows update killed a work session; every file came back perfectly, but Caleb still felt lost and disconnected when he returned.
nodes.why_it_exists: The forced restart proved files recovered but the user did not; this names the real unsolved problem.

nodes.label: Diagnostic failures
nodes.plain_meaning: When something breaks, the breakage usually points straight at what was missing. Failures are not just interruptions — they are how you discover what to build next.
nodes.emergence: Caleb noticed every big idea in this project had come from something going wrong, never from planned brainstorming.
nodes.why_it_exists: The forced restart, copy-paste fog, and convergence smoothing each exposed the layer needed to fix them.

nodes.label: The web already exists in git
nodes.plain_meaning: The full history of how this project grew is already recorded in its saved version history. Nobody has to build that history — it only needs to be shown.
nodes.emergence: Caleb wanted a visual map of how the ideas grew, and it turned out the record of that growth was already sitting in the project's saved history.
nodes.why_it_exists: Reframes the visualization job from 'create topology' to 'reveal topology that already exists'.

nodes.label: Recursive self-consistency warning
nodes.plain_meaning: A set of ideas can all fit together neatly and still be wrong. Before trusting how well it all connects, deliberately try to poke holes in it.
nodes.emergence: The ARIA ideas started fitting together so neatly it became tempting to trust them just for being consistent.
nodes.why_it_exists: The architecture started demonstrating itself recursively, which is seductive and needs a counterweight.

nodes.label: Convergence Trap
nodes.plain_meaning: When all three AIs agree right away, treat that as a warning sign, not proof — fast agreement can just mean they all share the same blind spot.
nodes.emergence: All three AIs once agreed on something instantly and were wrong; Caleb pushing back turned out to be the right call.
nodes.why_it_exists: All three AIs once agreed 'build it tonight' and were wrong; the user's pushback was right.

nodes.label: Loaded Witness
nodes.plain_meaning: Never reset all three AIs at the same time. Keep at least one of them still holding the live memory of the conversation, so nothing is lost.
nodes.emergence: A work session nearly lost all its context because too much was cleared at once.
nodes.why_it_exists: Clearing all AIs at once loses all warm context; one must stay loaded as a witness.

nodes.label: No Idea Wasted
nodes.plain_meaning: Save every idea the moment it shows up. Never decide an idea is not worth keeping while you are still capturing it — sort the strong from the weak later.
nodes.emergence: Ideas were getting filtered out as they were spoken, and sometimes the discarded one was the key to the best idea.
nodes.why_it_exists: A filtered idea that gets discarded might have been the connection point for the most important insight.
nodes.came_from: Locked as a foundational ARIA principle, 2026-05-11.

nodes.label: Self-defense first
nodes.plain_meaning: When two AIs compare answers, each should first say what is strongest in its own answer before judging the other's — so good points do not get dropped just to agree.
nodes.emergence: AIs kept just deferring to each other instead of defending their own good points, so real combining of ideas never happened.
nodes.why_it_exists: Without it, AIs collapse into 'use theirs' and real merges never happen.

nodes.label: LOCKED 37: Cross-AI Reconciliation
nodes.plain_meaning: When AIs disagree, they must either combine their answers or disagree out loud with reasons — never quietly give in.
nodes.emergence: Quiet giving-in between AIs was producing fake agreement and erasing the disagreements that carried the most insight.
nodes.why_it_exists: Mutual deference between AIs loses signal; synthesis or principled disagreement preserves it.

nodes.label: Idea Half-Life
nodes.plain_meaning: An idea saved for 60 days without being used gets a check-in: is this still relevant? Without that, the pile of saved ideas turns into a junk drawer nobody trusts.
nodes.emergence: The collection of saved ideas kept growing with no way to tell which ones were still alive.
nodes.why_it_exists: Without aging, the idea compost becomes a graveyard you stop trusting.

nodes.label: Round Closer / Resolution Anchor Pass
nodes.plain_meaning: When you have been copy-pasting answers between Claude, GPT, and Code for a while, this notices the question is already answered and tells you to stop — after saving the best bits from each AI.
nodes.emergence: Caleb got stuck copy-pasting between the AIs long after the question was answered, and good details were getting smoothed away each time.
nodes.why_it_exists: Cross-paste loops kept producing value while the user lost orientation and good details got smoothed away.
nodes.came_from: The 2026-05-12 session was itself the failure mode it describes.

nodes.label: Gem Flag (**)
nodes.plain_meaning: Mark a sentence with ** and it is promised to survive the next round of summarizing — or be dropped only with a stated reason.
nodes.emergence: Good specific phrasings kept vanishing when one AI's answer got summarized by the next.
nodes.why_it_exists: Important phrases get smoothed away in synthesis; an explicit marker forces keep-or-drop-with-reason.
nodes.came_from: Session marker convention; not yet a dedicated repo entry.

nodes.label: Park-this shorthand
nodes.plain_meaning: A quick way to set an idea aside without stopping to fully write it up or chase it down — it is saved, and you keep moving.
nodes.emergence: Capturing an idea mid-flow always meant breaking focus; Caleb wanted a way to save one without the interruption.
nodes.why_it_exists: Capturing an idea mid-flow should not require engaging a full sidequest; park-this is silent capture.

nodes.label: Blind Trio Round
nodes.plain_meaning: Ask each AI the same question separately, before any of them see the others' answers — so you get three genuinely independent takes instead of three that copied each other.
nodes.emergence: AIs reading each other too early produced samey answers and lost the value of independent thinking.
nodes.why_it_exists: Premature convergence flattens independent thinking; blind first-takes stay uncontaminated.
nodes.came_from: Designed and validated across the 2026-05-11/12 sessions.

nodes.label: Door Log
nodes.plain_meaning: A running list of the exact question that kicked off each major piece of work. You can always re-ask a question; you cannot always get back the feeling of an idea.
nodes.emergence: Caleb needed to navigate the project by the questions that started things, because questions were easier to remember than summaries.
nodes.why_it_exists: You can re-ask a question; you cannot re-feel an idea the same way twice.
nodes.came_from: Three-way convergence (Code/GPT/Claude), 2026-05-14; named the first buildable primitive.

nodes.label: Cognitive Mansion
nodes.plain_meaning: Picture your ideas as rooms in a house you can walk through — rooms are clusters of ideas, doorways are the questions that opened them, and how warm a room feels shows how active that thinking still is.
nodes.emergence: Caleb described his ideas as a huge mental mansion he needed to walk through and remember why he built each room.
nodes.why_it_exists: The user thinks spatially; a mansion you can walk through fits the brain better than a file tree.

nodes.label: Orientation Debt
nodes.plain_meaning: The slowly-building cost of losing track of where you are across many heavy work sessions. Nothing got deleted — you just do not feel on top of it anymore.
nodes.emergence: After heavy sessions Caleb's files were all fine but he still felt lost, and that feeling had no name.
nodes.why_it_exists: Names the silent thing that makes the user feel lost even when nothing was deleted.

nodes.label: Meaning Trails
nodes.plain_meaning: Not just 'this idea links to that idea' — the actual chain of why one idea led to the next. The route, not just the dots.
nodes.emergence: Plain links between ideas showed that things connected but lost the reason and the order they connected in.
nodes.why_it_exists: Plain links say 'this relates to that' but lose why; trails keep the causality.

nodes.label: Archaeology Mode vs Navigation Mode
nodes.plain_meaning: Two different ways to look at the project that should never be mixed: one shows how things actually happened, messy and with wrong turns; the other shows a clean plan for what to do next.
nodes.emergence: Mixing 'what happened' with 'what to do next' made past mistakes look like recommended steps.
nodes.why_it_exists: Preserving real emergence is a different job from showing clean forward paths; merging them destroys both.

nodes.label: Live-State Continuity (3-layer stack)
nodes.plain_meaning: A set of three tools that together protect not just your files but your sense of where you are — closing loops, checking you still feel oriented, and rebuilding that feeling when it is lost.
nodes.emergence: The forced restart showed that protecting files is not enough; the person's mental footing needs protecting too.
nodes.why_it_exists: Repo continuity is not enough; the warm human state needs its own preservation layer.
nodes.came_from: Merge of Round Closer + Comprehension Gate + Warm Reconstruction, 2026-05-14.

nodes.label: Scope differentiation
nodes.plain_meaning: Two answers that seem to disagree are often both right — they are just answering slightly different versions of the question. Name the two situations instead of picking a winner.
nodes.emergence: AIs looked like they were contradicting each other when they were really answering different-sized questions.
nodes.why_it_exists: Winner/loser thinking destroys valid ideas; scope-splitting preserves both.
nodes.came_from: Surfaced 2026-05-14; the behavior is produced by LOCKED 37 cross-AI reconciliation.

nodes.label: Collision Records
nodes.plain_meaning: On purpose, writing down 'one AI thought X, another disagreed with Y, here is what we settled on' — keeping the disagreement, not just the conclusion.
nodes.emergence: Disagreements between AIs carried the most insight but were getting erased the moment a conclusion was reached.
nodes.why_it_exists: Disagreement is where the load-bearing thinking happens; flattening it loses the most valuable part.

nodes.label: ARIA = Uncrumple (structurally identical)
nodes.plain_meaning: ARIA and the Uncrumple receipt app are the same shape of system in two different worlds — both take messy raw input and turn it into something organized and usable.
nodes.emergence: Caleb noticed the receipt app he was building and the thinking-system he was building had the exact same underlying structure.
nodes.why_it_exists: Both route messy evidence to usable decisions; the architecture transfers across domains.

nodes.label: Ghost Rooms
nodes.plain_meaning: Show the ideas that were started once and then forgotten — abandoned threads often hold the most important unfinished thinking.
nodes.emergence: Caleb realized he kept starting ideas and moving on, and wanted the forgotten ones surfaced instead of lost.
nodes.why_it_exists: Forgotten rooms often contain lost resonance and future breakthroughs.

nodes.label: Living Doors
nodes.plain_meaning: A question you asked once keeps gathering more ideas over time, until that single question becomes a whole area of its own.
nodes.emergence: Some early questions kept attracting new ideas months later — they were growing, not staying put.
nodes.why_it_exists: A question asked months ago keeps gathering meaning; the door becomes the room.

nodes.label: Thread Temperature
nodes.plain_meaning: Show how 'warm' each idea is — recently worked-on ideas glow, ideas left untouched for a long time visibly cool down — so you can see at a glance what is still active.
nodes.emergence: Every idea on the map looked equally alive, hiding which ones Caleb was actually still working on.
nodes.why_it_exists: Every node looking equally alive hides which threads are actually active versus cooling.
nodes.came_from: Claude, while building the first web prototype, 2026-05-15.

nodes.label: Ouroboros (ARIA needs ARIA)
nodes.plain_meaning: To build ARIA properly you would already need ARIA to keep the project organized. That loop is not a problem — doing it by hand right now is itself the proof the idea works.
nodes.emergence: Caleb's friend Mandy described it as a snake eating its own tail: you need the thing in order to build the thing.
nodes.why_it_exists: Building ARIA requires ARIA; the recursion is not a bug, it is the proof of concept.
nodes.came_from: Mandy's phrasing ('the snake eating itself'), via Caleb, 2026-05-14.

edges.why: Preserving emergence before compression is the thesis made operational — it is the specific discipline that stops cognition from being flattened into a lossy summary.
